opp.next_step: 6/18/26 MQ Neil responded to my query regarding next steps/timing stating Perdue will be assessing vendor responses and will re-connect in approx. 30 days. 6/1/26. MQ After telling Neil we weren't going to participate in RFI due to Direct Spend requirements, Neil and other members of the evaluation team clarified their position. The RFI contained DS requirements because other vendor's solutions included DS but the primary focus of the project was identifying the BOB S2P for Indirect Spend. Our RFI response has been submitted and Devica will be presenting scripted demo on 6/2. 5/27/26 MQ After reviewing Perdue's response to our clarification questions Devica and Bikash determined this project was not a fit from a functional perspective due to inclusion of direct spend for strategic sourcing, integration with LIMS and QMS (direct), and limited usage of MS Teams (Zoom chat). Reaching out to Perdue to let them know we will not be participating. 5/20/26 MQ Received RFI and facilitated MNDA execution. Sending RFI to SC for initial review. Supplier Q's due 5/22 resposes due 5/27
opp.ais_why:
* This is a formal RFI for a Source-to-Pay platform with conventional, functionality-driven requirements: "strategic sourcing functions including supplier discovery, RFx event management, bid analysis... and/or procure-to-pay functions including requisitioning, purchase order management... and integration with ERP systems." No AI priority is stated.
* Fit concerns cut against AI value: Zycus nearly declined over direct-spend/LIMS/QMS requirements and "limited usage of MS Teams (Zoom chat)" - the Teams limitation directly undercuts Merlin's conversational AI intake.
* The 6/2 session was a ~90-minute product demo; its topic segments and all 16 action items were reviewed - the customer's follow-ups are entirely functional/integration-driven (two- vs three-way match configuration, SAP-certified adapters/GRIR, manual invoice validation, and down-select use-case sessions), with no AI capability raised as a priority. Selection is S2P functional-fit-led, not AI-led. Formal Evaluation, 20%.

metrics.narrative: Perdue articulated qualitative pain around manual invoice validation processes and the need for a best-of-breed indirect S2P platform, but no quantified business case has been stated. Richard Morin (VP Corporate Controller) asked detailed questions about two-way vs three-way matching configuration and SAP integration during the 21 May demo, signaling operational pain in AP/invoice processing, but no cost, cycle-time, headcount, or ROI figures were shared. The RFI scope (iContract, iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, eInvoicing, plus Merlin AI) implies a broad transformation, yet no compelling event or financial target has been quantified. The 27 May Next Step notes that Devica and Bikash initially assessed the project as not a fit due to direct spend and LIMS/QMS integration requirements, but Perdue clarified 1 Jun that the primary focus is indirect spend, resolving the scope concern. Without a quantified value case, Zycus cannot anchor commercials or differentiate on ROI. Recommended move: during the mid-July reconnect, ask Neil or Richard to share the business case or savings target driving the RFI so Zycus can tailor the commercial proposal and ROI workshop to Perdue's financial goals.

champion.narrative: Neil Schloss is the identified champion, but his advocacy is developing and his role appears to be process coordinator rather than internal seller. Neil is the primary contact across all Next Step updates (20 May, 27 May, 1 Jun, 18 Jun) and he facilitated the RFI, clarified scope, and committed to reconnect mid-July. However, there is no evidence of Neil advocating FOR Zycus, blocking competitors, influencing the scorecard, or providing insider intelligence on how Zycus is rated vs rivals. His 1 Jun clarification that the RFI's direct spend requirements were included for other vendors (not Perdue's core need) suggests he is helpful and transparent, but this is process management, not championship. Neil has not introduced Zycus to Richard Morin (EB) or other executives, has not shared Perdue's internal evaluation criteria or competitive standing, and has not pushed back on competitors. He is engaged and responsive, but passive. The risk: if Neil is purely a procurement coordinator, he may lack the influence to position Zycus favorably in the down-select, especially if a rival has a stronger internal advocate. Recommended move: during mid-July reconnect, assess Neil's influence (does he have a vote in the down-select? does he report to Richard Morin or a CPO? who else on the evaluation team could champion Zycus?) and if Neil is not a true champion, identify and cultivate a second advocate with scorecard influence, ideally someone who values Zycus's AI/Merlin differentiation or has a relationship with Zycus from the past iContract deployment.

competition.narrative: The competitive field is partially known but critically incomplete. Agiloft CLM is confirmed: Perdue replaced Zycus iContract with Agiloft 3 years ago, so Agiloft owns the CLM relationship and may be defending or expanding into S2P. Maximo P2P is confirmed: the 21 May demo notes reference Maximo integration as a requirement, signaling that IBM Maximo (asset/maintenance management) is part of Perdue's tech stack, and Maximo P2P may be a competitor or integration partner. However, the core S2P competitive set is unknown. This is a multi-vendor RFI, but Zycus has no intelligence on who else is being evaluated (likely suspects: SAP Ariba, Coupa, GEP, Ivalua, Jaggaer, or Oracle). The 18 Jun Next Step states that Perdue is assessing vendor responses, but Zycus has no read on scorecard standing, how many vendors are in the RFI, or who is favored. The wedge: Zycus's AI/Merlin differentiation (Merlin Intake, Agentic AI in the product scope) and the ability to integrate with Agiloft CLM (protect their investment while filling S2P gaps) rather than rip-and-replace. However, without knowing the competitive field or Perdue's criteria weighting, Zycus cannot position the wedge effectively. High risk: a rival may lead on a criterion Zycus is unaware of (e.g. direct spend if another vendor can handle both, SAP certification depth, implementation speed, pricing). Recommended move: during mid-July reconnect, ask Neil who else is in the RFI, how many finalists Perdue expects to down-select, and how Zycus is currently rated vs the field. If Neil won't share, triangulate by asking what criteria Perdue is prioritizing and where Zycus needs to strengthen its position.

identify_pain.narrative: Perdue's pain is operationally confirmed but not financially quantified. Richard Morin (VP Corporate Controller) owns AP/invoice processing pain: his 21 May questions about manual invoice validation processes, two-way vs three-way matching configuration, and SAP integration signal that Perdue's current state is manual, error-prone, and tightly coupled to the relevant stakeholder 27 May Next Step notes that Perdue initially included direct spend, LIMS, and QMS integration requirements, suggesting procurement pain spans both direct and indirect categories, but the 1 Jun clarification narrowed scope to indirect S2P, indicating that indirect procurement (non-production goods/services) is the primary pain point. The RFI scope (iContract, iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, eInvoicing, Merlin AI) implies pain across the full source-to-pay lifecycle: manual sourcing, fragmented contract management (Perdue replaced Zycus iContract with Agiloft CLM 3 years ago, so CLM pain may persist), disconnected procurement and AP systems, and lack of AI-driven automation. However, no specific pain metrics (invoice processing cost, cycle time, error rate, sourcing event volume, contract leakage) have been quantified. The pain is real and owned by the right executive (Richard Morin, finance), but without quantification Zycus cannot anchor ROI or differentiate on business impact. Recommended move: ask Richard or Neil to share the current-state pain metrics (invoice volume, manual touch points, AP headcount, sourcing cycle time) so Zycus can build a quantified ROI case for the mid-July reconnect.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics, legal review, procurement channel, and InfoSec processes are completely unmapped. An MNDA was executed 20 May (Next Step 20 May), confirming that Perdue has a legal/procurement function, but no details on MSA/SOW requirements, standard terms, redline expectations, or InfoSec review timeline have been shared. The 21 May demo surfaced SAP integration and Maximo P2P integration as technical requirements, and Sajan Madabhushi (Senior Enterprise Architect, contact role) signals that IT/architecture will gate the deal, but no formal InfoSec or integration scoping process has been discussed. The 200-day close date (21 Jan 2027) assumes zero delay in contracting, but enterprise S2P deals typically require 60-90 days for MSA negotiation, SOW scoping, and InfoSec sign-off. If Perdue's down-select completes in Aug and commercials run Sep/Oct, contract must start by Nov to hit Jan close, leaving minimal buffer. Recommended move: during mid-July reconnect, ask Neil or Richard about Perdue's contract process (legal review timeline, InfoSec requirements, standard terms, any SI/implementation partner involvement) so Zycus can pre-stage MSA/SOW templates and InfoSec documentation to compress the post-commercial phase.

decision_process.narrative: Perdue is running a formal multi-vendor RFI with a structured timeline: RFI received 20 May, supplier questions due 22 May, responses due 27 May, Zycus response submitted 1 Jun, scripted demo 2 Jun. Neil Schloss (primary contact) stated 18 Jun that Perdue is assessing vendor responses and will reconnect in ~30 days (mid-July expected) to share down-select results. This maps to a classic enterprise procurement process: RFI round → vendor assessment → down-select to 2-3 finalists → deeper demos/workshops → commercials → EB proposal. However, the approval chain is unmapped. Richard Morin (VP Corporate Controller) is the EB, but it is unknown whether he has final sign-off or reports to a CFO/CEO for budget approval. The 21 May demo attendee list (7 contact roles: Kenneth Atkinson, Ashley Towne, Richard Morin, Mark Passen, Jennifer Langley, Sajan Madabhushi, Christina Sandefur) suggests a cross-functional evaluation team (procurement, finance, IT/architecture), but their individual influence and the formal decision-maker are unconfirmed. The process is structured and moving, but Zycus lacks visibility into who makes the final call and what happens post-down-select. Recommended move: during mid-July reconnect, ask Neil to walk through the post-RFI process (down-select, finalist workshops, EB presentation, contract timeline) and confirm the approval chain.

decision_criteria.narrative: The RFI response and 2 Jun scripted demo imply that Perdue's evaluation criteria center on indirect S2P functional coverage (sourcing, contracting, procurement, invoicing), SAP ERP integration, AI/Merlin capabilities, and configuration flexibility (e.g. matching rules). Richard Morin's 21 May questions about two-way vs three-way matching thresholds and SAP partner certification suggest that AP automation and ERP integration depth are weighted criteria. The 1 Jun clarification that direct spend and LIMS/QMS integration are out of scope narrows the playing field to pure indirect S2P vendors, which favors Zycus. However, the actual RFI scorecard, weighted criteria, and how Perdue will judge vendors in the down-select are unknown. No RFP document, test-case scenarios, or formal evaluation rubric has been shared. The mid-July reconnect must surface: (1) what criteria Perdue is scoring on, (2) how Zycus is currently rated vs competitors, and (3) whether a deeper product workshop or use-case validation is needed to prove fit on high-weight criteria. Without this intelligence, Zycus is flying blind into the down-select.

hard.ais_why:
* This is a formal RFI for a Source-to-Pay platform with conventional, functionality-driven requirements: "strategic sourcing functions including supplier discovery, RFx event management, bid analysis... and/or procure-to-pay functions including requisitioning, purchase order management... and integration with ERP systems." No AI priority is stated.
* Fit concerns cut against AI value: Zycus nearly declined over direct-spend/LIMS/QMS requirements and "limited usage of MS Teams (Zoom chat)" - the Teams limitation directly undercuts Merlin's conversational AI intake.
* The 6/2 session was a ~90-minute product demo; its topic segments and all 16 action items were reviewed - the customer's follow-ups are entirely functional/integration-driven (two- vs three-way match configuration, SAP-certified adapters/GRIR, manual invoice validation, and down-select use-case sessions), with no AI capability raised as a priority. Selection is S2P functional-fit-led, not AI-led. Formal Evaluation, 20%.

deltas.subject: Competitive field is unknown — the single highest-risk gap. In a formal multi-vendor RFI with a scorecard down-select, we have no intelligence on who else is being evaluated, how many will be shortlisted, or where Zycus stands. Without this we cannot validate our AI differentiation wedge (Merlin/Anna) or position against rival strengths. Before the mid-July reconnect, reach out to Neil Schloss (or another evaluation team contact) to ask: (1) who else is in the RFI, (2) how many vendors will be shortlisted, (3) what are the weighted scorecard criteria, and (4) where does Zycus currently stand. This intel is essential to adjust messaging and block threats before the down-select is final.

deltas.subject: Champion is developing but not yet advocating internally. deltas.subject: Economic Buyer (Richard Morin, VP Corporate Controller) is engaged but not yet commercially aligned. Morin participated actively in the 2 Jun demo with technical and commercial questions, confirming his EB authority. However, we have not yet run a formal ROI or value workshop to align him on the financial case (savings, risk reduction, throughput gains). Once down-selected (mid-July), the immediate next move is a targeted ROI session with Morin and finance to quantify the business case (70% intake lift, AP cycle-time savings, compliance cost avoidance) and lock his sponsorship before contract and pricing negotiations begin. Without EB alignment on value, pricing conversations are weaker and slip risk is higher.
